koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|千葉県[[印西市]]にて撮影。 Image:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|岐阜県中津川市阿木字天神平 Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|青面金剛と三猿（千葉県[[習志野市]]屋敷） Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|百庚申（埼玉県[[深谷市]]岡地区） |[[東京都]][[新宿区]]、来迎寺の庚申塔。

[[画像:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|神奈川県[[藤沢市]] [[伊勢山 (藤沢市)|伊勢山]]公園にて、2017年8月18日撮影]] [[Image:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|栃木県[[日光市]]にて撮影]] '''庚申塔'''（こうしんとう）は、'''庚申塚'''（こうしんづか）ともいい、[[中国]]より伝来した[[道教]]に由来する[[庚申信仰]]に基づいて建てられた石塔のこと。[[庚申講]]を3年18回続けた記念に建立されることが多い。塚の上に石塔を建てることから'''庚申塚'''、塔の建立に際して[[供養]]を伴ったことから'''庚申供養塔'''とも呼ばれる。 [[庚申待]]（庚申講）とは、[[人間]]の体内にいるという[[三尸虫]]（さんしちゅう）という[[虫]]が、[[庚申]]の日の夜零時、十二支文化圏ではいわゆる「子の刻」に日が変わる、とするのは[[近代]]の概念で、以前は限られた知識層（たとえば本居宣長が、家族に自分が未明に亡くなった場合には、命日は翌日である旨をわざわざ注意している）以外は、翌日の日の出をもって日が変わると考えるのが一般的であったことに注意。寝ている間に[[天帝]]にその人間の悪事を報告しに行くとされていることから、それを避けるためとして庚申の日の夜は夜通し眠らないで天帝や[[猿田彦]]や馬頭観音や青面金剛を祀り、勤行をしたり宴会をしたりする風習である。 庚申塔の石形や彫られる仏像、神像、文字などはさまざまであるが、[[申]]は[[干支]]で猿に例えられるから、「見ざる、言わざる、聞かざる」の[[三猿]]を彫り、村の名前や庚申講員の氏名を記したものが多い。[[仏教]]では、庚申の本尊は[[青面金剛]]とされるため、青面金剛が彫られることもある。[[神道]]では[[猿田彦神]]とされ、猿田彦神が彫られることもある。また、庚申塔には街道沿いに置かれ、塔に道標を彫り付けられたものも多い。さらに、[[道祖神|塞神]]として建立されることもあり、村の境目に建立されることもあった。 庚申塔は[[沖縄県]]を除く全国で分布が確認されているが、地域によって建立数に差が見られる。 例えば[[関東地方]]では数多く建立されているが、日本における庚申信仰の中心的な寺社がある[[京都]]や[[大阪]]など[[関西]]では庚申塔の建立は比較的少ない傾向がある。確認されている現存最古の庚申塔は[[埼玉県]]にある庚申板碑で[[文明 (日本)|文明]]3年（[[1471年]]）であり、当初は板碑や石幢などが多い。青面金剛刻像は[[福井県]]にある[[正保]]4年（[[1647年]]）が現存最古とされている。なお、奈良東大寺所有の木像青面金剛は[[鎌倉時代]]の作とされている。 == 歴史 == 庚申塔の建立が広く行われるようになるのは、[[江戸時代]]初期（[[寛永]]期以降）頃からである。以降、近世を通して多数の庚申塔が建てられた。当初は青面金剛や三猿像のほか、阿弥陀、[[地蔵]]など主尊が定まっていない時期を経て、徐々に青面金剛像が主尊の主流となった。その後、江戸中期から後期にかけて「庚申塔」あるいは「庚申」と文字のみ彫り付ける形式が増加する。 [[兵庫県]][[豊岡市]][[但東町]]では、石造庚申塔が77基（1956年以前の[[合橋村]]35基、[[高橋村 (兵庫県)|高橋村]]21基、[[資母村]]21基）確認され、[[18世紀]]から[[20世紀]]初めに多く造られている但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7。 [[明治]]時代になると、政府は庚申信仰を迷信と位置付けて街道筋に置かれたものを中心にその撤去を進めた。さらに[[高度経済成長期]]以降に行われた街道の拡張整備工事によって残存した庚申塔のほとんどが撤去や移転されることになった。 現在、残存する庚申塔の多くは寺社の境内や私有地に移転されたものや、もともと交通量の少ない街道脇に置かれていたため開発による破壊を免れたものである。田舎町へ行くと、今でも道の交差している箇所や村落の入り口などに、「庚申」と彫られた石塔を全国で見ることができる。 == ギャラリー == Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|福岡県[[宇美町]]炭焼地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|福岡県[[糸島市]]三雲地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607272310.JPG|福岡県[[大野城市]]、[[牛頸川]]上流の東岸の一角に。 Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町奥山 Image:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町寺坂 Image:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町三原 Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷（国道426号沿い） Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷 Image:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町河本 画像:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|長野県[[塩尻市]] [[奈良井宿]]の庚申塚。画面中央やや右。 Image: koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|千葉県[[印西市]]にて撮影。 Image:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|岐阜県中津川市阿木字天神平 Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|青面金剛と三猿（千葉県[[習志野市]]屋敷） Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|百庚申（埼玉県[[深谷市]]岡地区） |[[東京都]][[新宿区]]、来迎寺の庚申塔。
== 3Dモデル == ファイル:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|八雲神社（静岡県静岡市清水区西里）にある庚申塔（明和七年(1770年)造立） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[庚申]]（[[干支]]のひとつ） * [[庚申信仰]] * [[庚申待]] * [[庚申堂]] * [[道祖神]] * [[甲子塔]] {{Reli-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうしんとう}} [[Category:道教]] [[Category:日本の民間信仰]] [[Category:信仰塔]] [[Category:塚]] [[Category:路傍の神仏]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-庚申塔-qq]] [[ja:庚申塔]][[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-庚申塔-qq]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:庚申塔]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:庚申塔}][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:庚申塔]][[Category:qq]][[sn:庚申塔]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:庚申塔]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:庚申塔]]

現在、残存する庚申塔の多くは寺社の境内や私有地に移転されたものや、もともと交通量の少ない街道脇に置かれていたため開発による破壊を免れたものである。田舎町へ行くと、今でも道の交差している箇所や村落の入り口などに、「庚申」と彫られた石塔を全国で見ることができる。 == ギャラリー == Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|福岡県[[宇美町]]炭焼地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|福岡県[[糸島市]]三雲地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607272310.JPG|福岡県[[大野城市]]、[[牛頸川]]上流の東岸の一角に。 Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町奥山 Image:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町寺坂 Image:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町三原 Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷（国道426号沿い） Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷 Image:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町河本 画像:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|長野県[[塩尻市]] [[奈良井宿]]の庚申塚。画面中央やや右。 Image: koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|千葉県[[印西市]]にて撮影。 Image:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|岐阜県中津川市阿木字天神平 Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|青面金剛と三猿（千葉県[[習志野市]]屋敷） Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|百庚申（埼玉県[[深谷市]]岡地区） |[[東京都]][[新宿区]]、来迎寺の庚申塔。
== 3Dモデル == ファイル:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|八雲神社（静岡県静岡市清水区西里）にある庚申塔（明和七年(1770年)造立） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[庚申]]（[[干支]]のひとつ） * [[庚申信仰]] * [[庚申待]] * [[庚申堂]] * [[道祖神]] * [[甲子塔]] {{Reli-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうしんとう}} [[Category:道教]] [[Category:日本の民間信仰]] [[Category:信仰塔]] [[Category:塚]] [[Category:路傍の神仏]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-庚申塔-qq]] [[ja:庚申塔]][[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-庚申塔-qq]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:庚申塔]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:庚申塔}][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:庚申塔]][[Category:qq]][[sn:庚申塔]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:庚申塔]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:庚申塔]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:庚申塔]]

[[画像:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|神奈川県[[藤沢市]] [[伊勢山 (藤沢市)|伊勢山]]公園にて、2017年8月18日撮影]] [[Image:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|栃木県[[日光市]]にて撮影]] '''庚申塔'''（こうしんとう）は、'''庚申塚'''（こうしんづか）ともいい、[[中国]]より伝来した[[道教]]に由来する[[庚申信仰]]に基づいて建てられた石塔のこと。[[庚申講]]を3年18回続けた記念に建立されることが多い。塚の上に石塔を建てることから'''庚申塚'''、塔の建立に際して[[供養]]を伴ったことから'''庚申供養塔'''とも呼ばれる。 [[庚申待]]（庚申講）とは、[[人間]]の体内にいるという[[三尸虫]]（さんしちゅう）という[[虫]]が、[[庚申]]の日の夜零時、十二支文化圏ではいわゆる「子の刻」に日が変わる、とするのは[[近代]]の概念で、以前は限られた知識層（たとえば本居宣長が、家族に自分が未明に亡くなった場合には、命日は翌日である旨をわざわざ注意している）以外は、翌日の日の出をもって日が変わると考えるのが一般的であったことに注意。寝ている間に[[天帝]]にその人間の悪事を報告しに行くとされていることから、それを避けるためとして庚申の日の夜は夜通し眠らないで天帝や[[猿田彦]]や馬頭観音や青面金剛を祀り、勤行をしたり宴会をしたりする風習である。 庚申塔の石形や彫られる仏像、神像、文字などはさまざまであるが、[[申]]は[[干支]]で猿に例えられるから、「見ざる、言わざる、聞かざる」の[[三猿]]を彫り、村の名前や庚申講員の氏名を記したものが多い。[[仏教]]では、庚申の本尊は[[青面金剛]]とされるため、青面金剛が彫られることもある。[[神道]]では[[猿田彦神]]とされ、猿田彦神が彫られることもある。また、庚申塔には街道沿いに置かれ、塔に道標を彫り付けられたものも多い。さらに、[[道祖神|塞神]]として建立されることもあり、村の境目に建立されることもあった。 庚申塔は[[沖縄県]]など[[南西諸島]]を除く全国で分布が確認されているが、地域によって建立数に差が見られる。 例えば[[関東地方]]では数多く建立されているが、日本における庚申信仰の中心的な寺社がある[[京都]]や[[大阪]]など[[関西]]では庚申塔の建立は比較的少ない傾向がある。確認されている現存最古の庚申塔は[[埼玉県]]にある庚申板碑で[[文明 (日本)|文明]]3年（[[1471年]]）であり、当初は板碑や石幢などが多い。青面金剛刻像は[[福井県]]にある[[正保]]4年（[[1647年]]）が現存最古とされている。なお、奈良東大寺所有の木像青面金剛は[[鎌倉時代]]の作とされている。 == 歴史 == 庚申塔の建立が広く行われるようになるのは、[[江戸時代]]初期（[[寛永]]期以降）頃からである。以降、近世を通して多数の庚申塔が建てられた。当初は青面金剛や三猿像のほか、阿弥陀、[[地蔵]]など主尊が定まっていない時期を経て、徐々に青面金剛像が主尊の主流となった。その後、江戸中期から後期にかけて「庚申塔」あるいは「庚申」と文字のみ彫り付ける形式が増加する。 [[兵庫県]][[豊岡市]][[但東町]]では、石造庚申塔が77基（1956年以前の[[合橋村]]35基、[[高橋村 (兵庫県)|高橋村]]21基、[[資母村]]21基）確認され、[[18世紀]]から[[20世紀]]初めに多く造られている但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7。 [[明治]]時代になると、政府は庚申信仰を迷信と位置付けて街道筋に置かれたものを中心にその撤去を進めた。さらに[[高度経済成長期]]以降に行われた街道の拡張整備工事によって残存した庚申塔のほとんどが撤去や移転されることになった。 現在、残存する庚申塔の多くは寺社の境内や私有地に移転されたものや、もともと交通量の少ない街道脇に置かれていたため開発による破壊を免れたものである。田舎町へ行くと、今でも道の交差している箇所や村落の入り口などに、「庚申」と彫られた石塔を全国で見ることができる。 == ギャラリー == Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|福岡県[[宇美町]]炭焼地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|福岡県[[糸島市]]三雲地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607272310.JPG|福岡県[[大野城市]]、[[牛頸川]]上流の東岸の一角に。 Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町奥山 Image:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町寺坂 Image:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町三原 Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷（国道426号沿い） Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷 Image:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町河本 画像:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|長野県[[塩尻市]] [[奈良井宿]]の庚申塚。画面中央やや右。 Image: koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|千葉県[[印西市]]にて撮影。 Image:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|岐阜県中津川市阿木字天神平 Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|青面金剛と三猿（千葉県[[習志野市]]屋敷） Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|百庚申（埼玉県[[深谷市]]岡地区） |[[東京都]][[新宿区]]、来迎寺の庚申塔。
== 3Dモデル == ファイル:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|八雲神社（静岡県静岡市清水区西里）にある庚申塔（明和七年(1770年)造立） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[庚申]]（[[干支]]のひとつ） * [[庚申信仰]] * [[庚申待]] * [[庚申堂]] * [[道祖神]] * [[甲子塔]] {{Reli-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうしんとう}} [[Category:道教]] [[Category:日本の民間信仰]] [[Category:信仰塔]] [[Category:塚]] [[Category:庚申信仰]] [[Category:路傍の神仏]]

[[画像:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|神奈川県[[藤沢市]] [[伊勢山 (藤沢市)|伊勢山]]公園にて、2017年8月18日撮影]] [[Image:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|栃木県[[日光市]]にて撮影]] '''庚申塔'''（こうしんとう）は、'''庚申塚'''（こうしんづか）ともいい、[[中国]]より伝来した[[道教]]に由来する[[庚申信仰]]に基づいて建てられた石塔のこと。[[庚申講]]を3年18回続けた記念に建立されることが多い。塚の上に石塔を建てることから'''庚申塚'''、塔の建立に際して[[供養]]を伴ったことから'''庚申供養塔'''とも呼ばれる。 [[庚申待]]（庚申講）とは、[[人間]]の体内にいるという[[三尸虫]]（さんしちゅう）という[[虫]]が、[[庚申]]の日の夜零時、十二支文化圏ではいわゆる「子の刻」に日が変わる、とするのは[[近代]]の概念で、以前は限られた知識層（たとえば本居宣長が、家族に自分が未明に亡くなった場合には、命日は翌日である旨をわざわざ注意している）以外は、翌日の日の出をもって日が変わると考えるのが一般的であったことに注意。寝ている間に[[天帝]]にその人間の悪事を報告しに行くとされていることから、それを避けるためとして庚申の日の夜は夜通し眠らないで天帝や[[猿田彦]]や馬頭観音や青面金剛を祀り、勤行をしたり宴会をしたりする風習である。 庚申塔の石形や彫られる仏像、神像、文字などはさまざまであるが、[[申]]は[[干支]]で猿に例えられるから、「見ざる、言わざる、聞かざる」の[[三猿]]を彫り、村の名前や庚申講員の氏名を記したものが多い。[[仏教]]では、庚申の本尊は[[青面金剛]]とされるため、青面金剛が彫られることもある。[[神道]]では[[猿田彦神]]とされ、猿田彦神が彫られることもある。また、庚申塔には街道沿いに置かれ、塔に道標を彫り付けられたものも多い。さらに、[[道祖神|塞神]]として建立されることもあり、村の境目に建立されることもあった。 庚申塔は[[沖縄県]]を除く全国で分布が確認されているが、地域によって建立数に差が見られる。 例えば[[関東地方]]では数多く建立されているが、日本における庚申信仰の中心的な寺社がある[[京都]]や[[大阪]]など[[関西]]では庚申塔の建立は比較的少ない傾向がある。確認されている現存最古の庚申塔は[[埼玉県]]にある庚申板碑で[[文明 (日本)|文明]]3年（[[1471年]]）であり、当初は板碑や石幢などが多い。青面金剛刻像は[[福井県]]にある[[正保]]4年（[[1647年]]）が現存最古とされている。なお、奈良東大寺所有の木像青面金剛は[[鎌倉時代]]の作とされている。 == History == 庚申塔の建立が広く行われるようになるのは、[[江戸時代]]初期（[[寛永]]期以降）頃からである。以降、近世を通して多数の庚申塔が建てられた。当初は青面金剛や三猿像のほか、阿弥陀、[[地蔵]]など主尊が定まっていない時期を経て、徐々に青面金剛像が主尊の主流となった。その後、江戸中期から後期にかけて「庚申塔」あるいは「庚申」と文字のみ彫り付ける形式が増加する。 [[兵庫県]][[豊岡市]][[但東町]]では、石造庚申塔が77基（1956年以前の[[合橋村]]35基、[[高橋村 (兵庫県)|高橋村]]21基、[[資母村]]21基）確認され、[[18世紀]]から[[20世紀]]初めに多く造られている但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7。 [[明治]]時代になると、政府は庚申信仰を迷信と位置付けて街道筋に置かれたものを中心にその撤去を進めた。さらに[[高度経済成長期]]以降に行われた街道の拡張整備工事によって残存した庚申塔のほとんどが撤去や移転されることになった。 現在、残存する庚申塔の多くは寺社の境内や私有地に移転されたものや、もともと交通量の少ない街道脇に置かれていたため開発による破壊を免れたものである。田舎町へ行くと、今でも道の交差している箇所や村落の入り口などに、「庚申」と彫られた石塔を全国で見ることができる。 == ギャラリー == Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|福岡県[[宇美町]]炭焼地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|福岡県[[糸島市]]三雲地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607272310.JPG|福岡県[[大野城市]]、[[牛頸川]]上流の東岸の一角に。 Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町奥山 Image:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町寺坂 Image:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町三原 Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷（国道426号沿い） Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷 Image:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町河本 画像:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|長野県[[塩尻市]] [[奈良井宿]]の庚申塚。画面中央やや右。 Image: koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|千葉県[[印西市]]にて撮影。 Image:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|岐阜県中津川市阿木字天神平 Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|青面金剛と三猿（千葉県[[習志野市]]屋敷） Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|百庚申（埼玉県[[深谷市]]岡地区） |[[東京都]][[新宿区]]、来迎寺の庚申塔。

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|T

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|T

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-kō|ja|庚申講}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-kō|ja|庚申講}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明}} 3 (1471). Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

File:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|At Terasaka, Izushi, Toyooka. File:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|At Mihara, {{ill|Tantō, Hyōgo|ja|但東町}}, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|Along National Route 426 in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|Upstream in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|At Kawamoto, Tantō, Toyooka. File:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|A Kōshin-zuka at {{ill|Narai-juku|ja|奈良井宿}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|ja|塩尻市}}. File:koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|Photographed in {{ill|Inzai, Chiba|ja|印西市}}. File:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|At Aki, {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu. File:Koshin pillar.jpg|Shōmen Kongō and Three Monkeys ({{ill|Narashino, Chiba|ja|習志野市}}, Chiba). File:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|{{ill|Hyaku Kōshin|ja|百庚申}} in Oka, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市}}, Saitama. File:東京都新宿区 来迎寺の庚申塔.jpg|Kōshin-tō at {{ill|Raikō-ji (Shinjuku)|ja|来迎寺}}, {{ill|Shinjuku, Tokyo|ja|新宿区}}. </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō at {{ill|Yakumo Shrine (Shizuoka)|ja|八雲神社}}, {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|ja|静岡市清水区}}, built in 1770 (Meiwa 7). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]

During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|Upstream in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|At Kawamoto, Tantō, Toyooka. revision.text:
== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|12=draft}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|12=draft}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|12=draft}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|12=draft}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=draft}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=draft}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=draft}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|12=draft}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=draft}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=draft}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|12=draft}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|12=draft}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|12=draft}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|12=draft}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=draft}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=draft}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=draft}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|12=draft}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=draft}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|12=draft}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|12=draft}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|12=draft}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|12=draft}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=draft}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=draft}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=draft}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

File:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|At a corner of Mikumo district, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市|12=draft}}, Fukuoka. File:庚申塔 UP200607272310.JPG|Along the upper east bank of {{ill|Ushikubi River|ja|牛頸川|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōnojō, Fukuoka|ja|大野城市|12=draft}}. File:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|At Okuyama, {{ill|Izushi, Hyōgo|ja|出石町|12=draft}}, Toyooka. File:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|At Terasaka, Izushi, Toyooka. File:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|At Mihara, {{ill|Tantō, Hyōgo|ja|但東町|12=draft}}, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|Along National Route 426 in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|Upstream in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|At Kawamoto, Tantō, Toyooka. File:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|A Kōshin-zuka at {{ill|Narai-juku|ja|奈良井宿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|ja|塩尻市|12=draft}}. File:koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|Photographed in {{ill|Inzai, Chiba|ja|印西市|12=draft}}. File:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|At Aki, {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市|12=draft}}, Gifu. File:Koshin pillar.jpg|Shōmen Kongō and Three Monkeys ({{ill|Narashino, Chiba|ja|習志野市|12=draft}}, Chiba). File:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|{{ill|Hyaku Kōshin|ja|百庚申|12=draft}} in Oka, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|12=draft}}, Saitama. File:東京都新宿区 来迎寺の庚申塔.jpg|Kōshin-tō at {{ill|Raikō-ji (Shinjuku)|ja|来迎寺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinjuku, Tokyo|ja|新宿区|12=draft}}. </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō at {{ill|Yakumo Shrine (Shizuoka)|ja|八雲神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|ja|静岡市清水区|12=draft}}, built in 1770 (Meiwa 7). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|12=draft}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|12=draft}}]]

By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=draft}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=draft}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

[[sn:Kōshin-tō]] [[ja:庚申塔]]

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

File:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|At a corner of Mikumo district, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka. File:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|At Terasaka, Izushi, Toyooka. File:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|At Mihara, {{ill|Tantō, Hyōgo|ja|但東町}}, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|Along National Route 426 in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|Upstream in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|At Kawamoto, Tantō, Toyooka. revision.comment: 81 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:庚申塔 up to rev 103910840
revision.text:
[[画像:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|神奈川県[[藤沢市]] [[伊勢山 (藤沢市)|伊勢山]]公園にて、2017年8月18日撮影]] [[Image:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|栃木県[[日光市]]にて撮影]] '''庚申塔'''（こうしんとう）は、'''庚申塚'''（こうしんづか）ともいい、[[中国]]より伝来した[[道教]]に由来する[[庚申信仰]]に基づいて建てられた石塔のこと。[[庚申講]]を3年18回続けた記念に建立されることが多い。塚の上に石塔を建てることから'''庚申塚'''、塔の建立に際して[[供養]]を伴ったことから'''庚申供養塔'''とも呼ばれる。 [[庚申待]]（庚申講）とは、[[人間]]の体内にいるという[[三尸虫]]（さんしちゅう）という[[虫]]が、[[庚申]]の日の夜零時、十二支文化圏ではいわゆる「子の刻」に日が変わる、とするのは[[近代]]の概念で、以前は限られた知識層（たとえば本居宣長が、家族に自分が未明に亡くなった場合には、命日は翌日である旨をわざわざ注意している）以外は、翌日の日の出をもって日が変わると考えるのが一般的であったことに注意。寝ている間に[[天帝]]にその人間の悪事を報告しに行くとされていることから、それを避けるためとして庚申の日の夜は夜通し眠らないで天帝や[[猿田彦]]や馬頭観音や青面金剛を祀り、勤行をしたり宴会をしたりする風習である。 庚申塔の石形や彫られる仏像、神像、文字などはさまざまであるが、[[申]]は[[干支]]で猿に例えられるから、「見ざる、言わざる、聞かざる」の[[三猿]]を彫り、村の名前や庚申講員の氏名を記したものが多い。[[仏教]]では、庚申の本尊は[[青面金剛]]とされるため、青面金剛が彫られることもある。[[神道]]では[[猿田彦神]]とされ、猿田彦神が彫られることもある。また、庚申塔には街道沿いに置かれ、塔に道標を彫り付けられたものも多い。さらに、[[道祖神|塞神]]として建立されることもあり、村の境目に建立されることもあった。 庚申塔は[[沖縄県]]など[[南西諸島]]を除く全国で分布が確認されているが、地域によって建立数に差が見られる。 例えば[[関東地方]]では数多く建立されているが、日本における庚申信仰の中心的な寺社がある[[京都]]や[[大阪]]など[[関西]]では庚申塔の建立は比較的少ない傾向がある。確認されている現存最古の庚申塔は[[埼玉県]]にある庚申板碑で[[文明 (日本)|文明]]3年（[[1471年]]）であり、当初は板碑や石幢などが多い。青面金剛刻像は[[福井県]]にある[[正保]]4年（[[1647年]]）が現存最古とされている。なお、奈良東大寺所有の木像青面金剛は[[鎌倉時代]]の作とされている。 == 歴史 == 庚申塔の建立が広く行われるようになるのは、[[江戸時代]]初期（[[寛永]]期以降）頃からである。以降、近世を通して多数の庚申塔が建てられた。当初は青面金剛や三猿像のほか、阿弥陀、[[地蔵]]など主尊が定まっていない時期を経て、徐々に青面金剛像が主尊の主流となった。その後、江戸中期から後期にかけて「庚申塔」あるいは「庚申」と文字のみ彫り付ける形式が増加する。 [[兵庫県]][[豊岡市]][[但東町]]では、石造庚申塔が77基（1956年以前の[[合橋村]]35基、[[高橋村 (兵庫県)|高橋村]]21基、[[資母村]]21基）確認され、[[18世紀]]から[[20世紀]]初めに多く造られている但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7。 [[明治]]時代になると、政府は庚申信仰を迷信と位置付けて街道筋に置かれたものを中心にその撤去を進めた。さらに[[高度経済成長期]]以降に行われた街道の拡張整備工事によって残存した庚申塔のほとんどが撤去や移転されることになった。 現在、残存する庚申塔の多くは寺社の境内や私有地に移転されたものや、もともと交通量の少ない街道脇に置かれていたため開発による破壊を免れたものである。田舎町へ行くと、今でも道の交差している箇所や村落の入り口などに、「庚申」と彫られた石塔を全国で見ることができる。 == ギャラリー == Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|福岡県[[宇美町]]炭焼地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|福岡県[[糸島市]]三雲地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607272310.JPG|福岡県[[大野城市]]、[[牛頸川]]上流の東岸の一角に。 Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町奥山 Image:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町寺坂 Image:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町三原 Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷（国道426号沿い） Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷 Image:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町河本 画像:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|長野県[[塩尻市]] [[奈良井宿]]の庚申塚。画面中央やや右。 Image: koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|千葉県[[印西市]]にて撮影。 Image:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|岐阜県中津川市阿木字天神平 Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|青面金剛と三猿（千葉県[[習志野市]]屋敷） Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|百庚申（埼玉県[[深谷市]]岡地区） |[[東京都]][[新宿区]]、来迎寺の庚申塔。
== 3Dモデル == ファイル:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|八雲神社（静岡県静岡市清水区西里）にある庚申塔（明和七年(1770年)造立） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[庚申]]（[[干支]]のひとつ） * [[庚申信仰]] * [[庚申待]] * [[庚申堂]] * [[道祖神]] * [[甲子塔]] {{Reli-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうしんとう}} [[Category:道教]] [[Category:日本の民間信仰]] [[Category:信仰塔]] [[Category:塚]] [[Category:庚申信仰]] [[Category:路傍の神仏]]

庚申塔の石形や彫られる仏像、神像、文字などはさまざまであるが、[[申]]は[[干支]]で猿に例えられるから、「見ざる、言わざる、聞かざる」の[[三猿]]を彫り、村の名前や庚申講員の氏名を記したものが多い。[[仏教]]では、庚申の本尊は[[青面金剛]]とされるため、青面金剛が彫られることもある。[[神道]]では[[猿田彦神]]とされ、猿田彦神が彫られることもある。また、庚申塔には街道沿いに置かれ、塔に道標を彫り付けられたものも多い。さらに、[[道祖神|塞神]]として建立されることもあり、村の境目に建立されることもあった。 庚申塔は[[沖縄県]]など[[南西諸島]]を除く全国で分布が確認されているが、地域によって建立数に差が見られる。 例えば[[関東地方]]では数多く建立されているが、日本における庚申信仰の中心的な寺社がある[[京都]]や[[大阪]]など[[関西]]では庚申塔の建立は比較的少ない傾向がある。確認されている現存最古の庚申塔は[[埼玉県]]にある庚申板碑で[[文明 (日本)|文明]]3年（[[1471年]]）であり、当初は板碑や石幢などが多い。青面金剛刻像は[[福井県]]にある[[正保]]4年（[[1647年]]）が現存最古とされている。なお、奈良東大寺所有の木像青面金剛は[[鎌倉時代]]の作とされている。 == 歴史 == 庚申塔の建立が広く行われるようになるのは、[[江戸時代]]初期（[[寛永]]期以降）頃からである。以降、近世を通して多数の庚申塔が建てられた。当初は青面金剛や三猿像のほか、阿弥陀、[[地蔵]]など主尊が定まっていない時期を経て、徐々に青面金剛像が主尊の主流となった。その後、江戸中期から後期にかけて「庚申塔」あるいは「庚申」と文字のみ彫り付ける形式が増加する。 [[兵庫県]][[豊岡市]][[但東町]]では、石造庚申塔が77基（1956年以前の[[合橋村]]35基、[[高橋村 (兵庫県)|高橋村]]21基、[[資母村]]21基）確認され、[[18世紀]]から[[20世紀]]初めに多く造られている但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999年、pp.6-7。 [[明治]]時代になると、政府は庚申信仰を迷信と位置付けて街道筋に置かれたものを中心にその撤去を進めた。さらに[[高度経済成長期]]以降に行われた街道の拡張整備工事によって残存した庚申塔のほとんどが撤去や移転されることになった。 現在、残存する庚申塔の多くは寺社の境内や私有地に移転されたものや、もともと交通量の少ない街道脇に置かれていたため開発による破壊を免れたものである。田舎町へ行くと、今でも道の交差している箇所や村落の入り口などに、「庚申」と彫られた石塔を全国で見ることができる。 == ギャラリー == Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|福岡県[[宇美町]]炭焼地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|福岡県[[糸島市]]三雲地区の一角に。 Image:庚申塔 UP200607272310.JPG|福岡県[[大野城市]]、[[牛頸川]]上流の東岸の一角に。 Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町奥山 Image:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市出石町寺坂 Image:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町三原 Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷（国道426号沿い） Image:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町小谷 Image:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|兵庫県豊岡市但東町河本 画像:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|長野県[[塩尻市]] [[奈良井宿]]の庚申塚。画面中央やや右。 Image: koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|千葉県[[印西市]]にて撮影。 Image:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|岐阜県中津川市阿木字天神平 Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|青面金剛と三猿（千葉県[[習志野市]]屋敷） Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|百庚申（埼玉県[[深谷市]]岡地区） |[[東京都]][[新宿区]]、来迎寺の庚申塔。
== 3Dモデル == ファイル:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|八雲神社（静岡県静岡市清水区西里）にある庚申塔（明和七年(1770年)造立） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[庚申]]（[[干支]]のひとつ） * [[庚申信仰]] * [[庚申待]] * [[庚申堂]] * [[道祖神]] * [[甲子塔]] {{Reli-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうしんとう}} [[Category:道教]] [[Category:日本の民間信仰]] [[Category:信仰塔]] [[Category:塚]] [[Category:庚申信仰]] [[Category:路傍の神仏]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
== Gallery == <gallery> Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Umi, Fukuoka]]. Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Itoshima, Fukuoka]]. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in [[Izushi, Hyōgo]]. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ([[Fukaya, Saitama]]). </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō from [[Yagumo Shrine]] (1770, [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|12=draft}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|12=draft}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|12=draft}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|12=draft}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=draft}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=draft}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=draft}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|12=draft}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=draft}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=draft}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|12=draft}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|12=draft}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|12=draft}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|12=draft}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=draft}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=draft}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=draft}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|12=draft}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=draft}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|12=draft}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|12=draft}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|12=draft}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|12=draft}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=draft}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=draft}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=draft}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|At a corner of Sumiyaki district, {{ill|Umi, Fukuoka|ja|宇美町|12=draft}}, Fukuoka. File:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|At a corner of Mikumo district, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市|12=draft}}, Fukuoka. File:庚申塔 UP200607272310.JPG|Along the upper east bank of {{ill|Ushikubi River|ja|牛頸川|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōnojō, Fukuoka|ja|大野城市|12=draft}}. File:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|At Okuyama, {{ill|Izushi, Hyōgo|ja|出石町|12=draft}}, Toyooka. File:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|At Terasaka, Izushi, Toyooka. File:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|At Mihara, {{ill|Tantō, Hyōgo|ja|但東町|12=draft}}, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|Along National Route 426 in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|Upstream in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|At Kawamoto, Tantō, Toyooka. File:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|A Kōshin-zuka at {{ill|Narai-juku|ja|奈良井宿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|ja|塩尻市|12=draft}}. File:koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|Photographed in {{ill|Inzai, Chiba|ja|印西市|12=draft}}. File:Miyata tenjinjinja03.jpg|At Aki, {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市|12=draft}}, Gifu. File:Koshin pillar.jpg|Shōmen Kongō and Three Monkeys ({{ill|Narashino, Chiba|ja|習志野市|12=draft}}, Chiba). File:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|{{ill|Hyaku Kōshin|ja|百庚申|12=draft}} in Oka, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|12=draft}}, Saitama. File:東京都新宿区 来迎寺の庚申塔.jpg|Kōshin-tō at {{ill|Raikō-ji (Shinjuku)|ja|来迎寺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinjuku, Tokyo|ja|新宿区|12=draft}}. </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō at {{ill|Yakumo Shrine (Shizuoka)|ja|八雲神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|ja|静岡市清水区|12=draft}}, built in 1770 (Meiwa 7). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|qq=draft}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|qq=draft}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|qq=draft}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|qq=draft}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
== Gallery == <gallery> Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Umi, Fukuoka]]. Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Itoshima, Fukuoka]]. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in [[Izushi, Hyōgo]]. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ([[Fukaya, Saitama]]). </gallery>

'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft}} over three years. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.

Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.
