== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|qq=}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa, Kanagawa|qq=}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō, Tochigi|qq=}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|qq=}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentei|qq=}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|qq=}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|qq=}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin (申)|qq=}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Wise Monkeys|qq=}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|qq=}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei (Japan)|qq=}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|qq=}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui Prefecture|qq=}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōhō (Japan)|qq=}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdai-ji|qq=}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'ei era|qq=}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
== Gallery == <gallery> Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Umi, Fukuoka]]. Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Itoshima, Fukuoka]]. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in [[Izushi, Hyōgo]]. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ([[Fukaya, Saitama]]). </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō from [[Yagumo Shrine]] (1770, [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]). </gallery>

In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

[[sn:Kōshin-tō]] [[ja:庚申塔]] [[sn:ōshintō]] [[en:Kōshin-tō]]
<!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō-->

{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|qq=}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa, Kanagawa|qq=}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō, Tochigi|qq=}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|qq=}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentei|qq=}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|qq=}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|qq=}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|1=Monkey (Zodiac)|lt=Monkey (申)|2=ja|3=申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin (申)|qq=}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Wise Monkeys|qq=}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|qq=}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei (Japan)|qq=}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|qq=}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui Prefecture|qq=}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōhō (Japan)|qq=}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdai-ji|qq=}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'ei era|qq=}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
== Gallery == <gallery> Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Umi, Fukuoka]]. Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Itoshima, Fukuoka]]. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in [[Izushi, Hyōgo]]. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ([[Fukaya, Saitama]]). </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō from [[Yagumo Shrine]] (1770, [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|qq=}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa, Kanagawa|qq=}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō, Tochigi|qq=}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|qq=}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentei|qq=}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|qq=}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|qq=}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|1=Monkey (Zodiac)|lt=Monkey (申)|2=ja|3=申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin (申)|qq=}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Wise Monkeys|qq=}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|qq=}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei (Japan)|qq=}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|qq=}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui Prefecture|qq=}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōhō (Japan)|qq=}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdai-ji|qq=}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'ei era|qq=}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

File:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|At a corner of Mikumo district, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itoshima, Fukuoka|qq=}}, Fukuoka. File:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|At Okuyama, {{ill|Izushi, Hyōgo|ja|出石町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izushi, Hyōgo|qq=}}, Toyooka. File:出石町寺坂の庚申塔.jpg|At Terasaka, Izushi, Toyooka. File:奈良井宿庚申塚・水場・重伝建選定碑.jpg|A Kōshin-zuka at {{ill|Narai-juku|ja|奈良井宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narai-juku|qq=}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|ja|塩尻市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiojiri, Nagano|qq=}}. File:koushindsuka_chiba_imba.jpg|Photographed in {{ill|Inzai, Chiba|ja|印西市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inzai, Chiba|qq=}}. File:Koshin pillar.jpg|Shōmen Kongō and Three Monkeys ({{ill|Narashino, Chiba|ja|習志野市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narashino, Chiba|qq=}}, Chiba). File:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|{{ill|Hyaku Kōshin|ja|百庚申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyaku Kōshin|qq=}} in Oka, {{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|ja|深谷市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukaya, Saitama|qq=}}, Saitama. File:東京都新宿区 来迎寺の庚申塔.jpg|Kōshin-tō at {{ill|Raikō-ji (Shinjuku)|ja|来迎寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raikō-ji (Shinjuku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Shinjuku, Tokyo|ja|新宿区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjuku, Tokyo|qq=}}. </gallery>

Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).

To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.

Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|qq=}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa, Kanagawa|qq=}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō, Tochigi|qq=}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} to Japan. {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|qq=}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentei|qq=}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|qq=}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|qq=}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|1=Monkey (Zodiac)|lt=Monkey (申)|2=ja|3=申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin (申)|qq=}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Wise Monkeys|qq=}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|qq=}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei (Japan)|qq=}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|qq=}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui Prefecture|qq=}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōhō (Japan)|qq=}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdai-ji|qq=}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'ei era|qq=}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

[[sn:Kōshin-tō]] [[ja:庚申塔]] [[sn:ōshintō]] [[en:Kōshin-tō]]
<!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō-->

{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|qq=}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa, Kanagawa|qq=}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō, Tochigi|qq=}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|qq=}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentei|qq=}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|qq=}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|qq=}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|1=Monkey (Zodiac)|lt=Monkey (申)|2=ja|3=申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin (申)|qq=}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Wise Monkeys|qq=}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|qq=}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei (Japan)|qq=}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|qq=}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui Prefecture|qq=}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōhō (Japan)|qq=}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdai-ji|qq=}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.

Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. [[sn:Kōshin-tō]] [[ja:庚申塔]] [[sn:ōshintō]] [[en:Kōshin-tō]]
<!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō-->

{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|qq=}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa, Kanagawa|qq=}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō, Tochigi|qq=}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|qq=}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentei|qq=}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|qq=}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|qq=}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|1=Monkey (Zodiac)|lt=Monkey (申)|2=ja|3=申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin (申)|qq=}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Wise Monkeys|qq=}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|qq=}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei (Japan)|qq=}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|qq=}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui Prefecture|qq=}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōhō (Japan)|qq=}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdai-ji|qq=}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'ei era|qq=}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|qq=}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|ja|藤沢市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa, Kanagawa|qq=}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō, Tochigi|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō, Tochigi|qq=}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin|lt=Kōshin|lt=Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|qq=}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Tentei|ja|天帝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentei|qq=}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|qq=}}, {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|qq=}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Shin (申)|ja|申|1=Monkey (Zodiac)|lt=Monkey (申)|2=ja|3=申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin (申)|qq=}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Wise Monkeys|qq=}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|qq=}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, dating to {{ill|Bunmei (Japan)|ja|文明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunmei (Japan)|qq=}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|qq=}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui Prefecture|qq=}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō (Japan)|ja|正保|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōhō (Japan)|qq=}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdai-ji|qq=}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'ei era|qq=}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

[[sn:Kōshin-tō]] [[ja:庚申塔]] [[sn:ōshintō]] [[en:Kōshin-tō]]
<!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō-->

{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.

Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in [[Izushi, Hyōgo]]. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[Narashino, Chiba]]). == History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei era|ja|寛永|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'ei era|qq=}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include [[three wise monkeys]] ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[zodiac sign]] of the monkey ([[申]]), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as [[Dōsojin]] to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except [[Okinawa Prefecture]], with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the [[Kantō region]], while they are less common in [[Kyoto]] and [[Osaka]] within the [[Kansai region]], despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from [[1471]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]]. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
== Gallery == <gallery> Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Umi, Fukuoka]]. Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in [[Itoshima, Fukuoka]]. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in [[Izushi, Hyōgo]]. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ([[Fukaya, Saitama]]). </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō from [[Yagumo Shrine]] (1770, [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|qq=}} in [[:en:Fujisawa, Kanagawa|Fujisawa, Kanagawa]], on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō, Tochigi]]]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], which originated in [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]] and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|qq=}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to [[:en:Shangdi|Tentei]] while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|qq=}}, [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]], or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|qq=}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign [[:en:Monkey (zodiac)|Monkey (申)]] is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Wise Monkeys]], along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|qq=}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|qq=}} from [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], dating to [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei (Japan)]] 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|qq=}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], dates to [[:en:Shōhō|Shōhō (Japan)]] 4 (1647). Additionally, [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], particularly after the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei era]]. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|qq=}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|qq=}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|qq=}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|qq=}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

File:但東町三原 庚申塔.jpg|At Mihara, [[:en:Tantō, Hyōgo|Tantō, Hyōgo]], Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（R426沿い）.jpg|Along National Route 426 in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町小谷の庚申塔（上流側）.jpg|Upstream in Kotani, Tantō, Toyooka. File:但東町河本の庚申塔.jpg|At Kawamoto, Tantō, Toyooka. File:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|{{ill|Hyaku Kōshin|ja|百庚申|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyaku Kōshin|qq=}} in Oka, [[:en:Fukaya, Saitama|Fukaya, Saitama]], Saitama. File:東京都新宿区 来迎寺の庚申塔.jpg|Kōshin-tō at {{ill|Raikō-ji (Shinjuku)|ja|来迎寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raikō-ji (Shinjuku)|qq=}}, [[:en:Shinjuku|Shinjuku, Tokyo]]. </gallery>

{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from [[China]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[Batō Kannon]], or [[Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.

[[Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in [[Buddhism]], or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. The earliest depiction of [[Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a [[Fukui Prefecture]] stone from [[1647]], while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the [[Kamakura period]].
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[Edo period]] (early [[17th century]]). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[Amida Buddha]] and [[Jizō]], but [[Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[Toyooka]]’s [[Tanto]] district. Most date from the [[18th century]] to the early [[20th century]]<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[Meiji period]], the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
