== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|1=Mount Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378991}} in [[:en:Fujisawa, Kanagawa|Fujisawa, Kanagawa]], on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō, Tochigi]]]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], which originated in [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]] and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申待}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三尸|1=Three Corpses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9380693}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to [[:en:Shangdi|Tentei]] while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090037}}, [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]], or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign [[:en:Monkey (zodiac)|Monkey (申)]] is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Wise Monkeys]], along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|1=itabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532699}} from [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], dating to [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei (Japan)]] 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], dates to [[:en:Shōhō|Shōhō (Japan)]] 4 (1647). Additionally, [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], particularly after the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei era]]. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|1=Aihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042878}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|1=Takahashi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11671340}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|1=Shimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20044858}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|1=Mount Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378991}} in [[:en:Fujisawa, Kanagawa|Fujisawa, Kanagawa]], on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō, Tochigi]]]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], which originated in [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]] and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申待}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三尸|1=Three Corpses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9380693}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to [[:en:Shangdi|Tentei]] while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090037}}, [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]], or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign [[:en:Monkey (zodiac)|Monkey (申)]] is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Wise Monkeys]], along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|1=itabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532699}} from [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], dating to [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei (Japan)]] 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], dates to [[:en:Shōhō|Shōhō (Japan)]] 4 (1647). Additionally, [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]] in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], particularly after the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei era]]. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|1=Aihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042878}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|1=Takahashi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11671340}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|1=Shimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20044858}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11486001]] Wikidata name: Kōshin-tō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[:en:Heavenly Emperor|Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[:en:Batō Kannon|Batō Kannon]], or [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three wise monkeys}} ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[:en:zodiac sign|zodiac sign]] of the monkey ({{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (zodiac)}}), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}} to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}}, while they are less common in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} within the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}, despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from {{ill|1471|en|1471|WD=Q6577|lt=1471}} in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. The earliest depiction of [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} stone from {{ill|1647|en|1647|WD=Q6935|lt=1647}}, while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (early {{ill|17th century|en|17th century|WD=Q7016|lt=17th century}}). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[:en:Amida Buddha|Amida Buddha]] and {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}, but [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[:en:Toyooka|Toyooka]]’s {{ill|Tanto|en|Tanto|WD=Q7683849|lt=Tanto}} district. Most date from the {{ill|18th century|en|18th century|WD=Q7015|lt=18th century}} to the early {{ill|20th century|en|20th century|WD=Q6927|lt=20th century}}<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[:en:postwar economic boom|postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
== Gallery == <gallery> Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Umi, Fukuoka|en|Umi, Fukuoka|WD=Q945518|lt=Umi, Fukuoka}}. Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima, Fukuoka|WD=Q1011145|lt=Itoshima, Fukuoka}}. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Izushi, Hyōgo|en|Izushi, Hyōgo|WD=Q6102453|lt=Izushi, Hyōgo}}. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[:en:Narashino, Chiba|Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ({{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|en|Fukaya, Saitama|WD=Q734532|lt=Fukaya, Saitama}}). </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō from [[Yagumo Shrine]] (1770, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|1=Mount Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378991}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|en|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|WD=Q386661|lt=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō, Tochigi}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin|en|Kōshin|WD=Q3082157|lt=Kōshin faith}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申待}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三尸|1=Three Corpses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9380693}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Shangdi|en|Shangdi|WD=Q2714875|lt=Tentei}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090037}}, {{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|en|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|WD=Q17004306|lt=Batō Kannon}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (申)}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three Wise Monkeys}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|1=itabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532699}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, dating to {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei (Japan)}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō|en|Shōhō|WD=Q1146192|lt=Shōhō (Japan)}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei era}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|1=Aihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042878}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|1=Takahashi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11671340}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|1=Shimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20044858}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11486001]] Wikidata name: Kōshin-tō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[:en:Heavenly Emperor|Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[:en:Batō Kannon|Batō Kannon]], or [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three wise monkeys}} ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[:en:zodiac sign|zodiac sign]] of the monkey ({{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (zodiac)}}), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}} to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}}, while they are less common in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} within the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}, despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from {{ill|1471|en|1471|WD=Q6577|lt=1471}} in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. The earliest depiction of [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} stone from {{ill|1647|en|1647|WD=Q6935|lt=1647}}, while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (early {{ill|17th century|en|17th century|WD=Q7016|lt=17th century}}). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[:en:Amida Buddha|Amida Buddha]] and {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}, but [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[:en:Toyooka|Toyooka]]’s {{ill|Tanto|en|Tanto|WD=Q7683849|lt=Tanto}} district. Most date from the {{ill|18th century|en|18th century|WD=Q7015|lt=18th century}} to the early {{ill|20th century|en|20th century|WD=Q6927|lt=20th century}}<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[:en:postwar economic boom|postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.

Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima, Fukuoka|WD=Q1011145|lt=Itoshima, Fukuoka}}. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Izushi, Hyōgo|en|Izushi, Hyōgo|WD=Q6102453|lt=Izushi, Hyōgo}}. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[:en:Narashino, Chiba|Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ({{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|en|Fukaya, Saitama|WD=Q734532|lt=Fukaya, Saitama}}). </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō from [[Yagumo Shrine]] (1770, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|1=Mount Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378991}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|en|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|WD=Q386661|lt=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō, Tochigi}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin|en|Kōshin|WD=Q3082157|lt=Kōshin faith}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申待}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三尸|1=Three Corpses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9380693}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Shangdi|en|Shangdi|WD=Q2714875|lt=Tentei}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090037}}, {{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|en|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|WD=Q17004306|lt=Batō Kannon}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (申)}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three Wise Monkeys}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|1=itabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532699}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, dating to {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei (Japan)}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō|en|Shōhō|WD=Q1146192|lt=Shōhō (Japan)}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei era}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|1=Aihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042878}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|1=Takahashi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11671340}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|1=Shimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20044858}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

[[ja:庚申塔]]
<!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--><!--Kōshin-tō--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kōshin-tō}} {{wikidata link|Q11486001}} [[Category: of the [[Kōshin vigil]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Mounds]] [[Category:Religious towers]] [[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Tumuli]] [[Category:Wayside Deities and Buddhas]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]]
{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[:en:Heavenly Emperor|Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[:en:Batō Kannon|Batō Kannon]], or [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three wise monkeys}} ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[:en:zodiac sign|zodiac sign]] of the monkey ({{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (zodiac)}}), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}} to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}}, while they are less common in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} within the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}, despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from {{ill|1471|en|1471|WD=Q6577|lt=1471}} in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. The earliest depiction of [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} stone from {{ill|1647|en|1647|WD=Q6935|lt=1647}}, while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (early {{ill|17th century|en|17th century|WD=Q7016|lt=17th century}}). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[:en:Amida Buddha|Amida Buddha]] and {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}, but [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[:en:Toyooka|Toyooka]]’s {{ill|Tanto|en|Tanto|WD=Q7683849|lt=Tanto}} district. Most date from the {{ill|18th century|en|18th century|WD=Q7015|lt=18th century}} to the early {{ill|20th century|en|20th century|WD=Q6927|lt=20th century}}<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[:en:postwar economic boom|postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|1=Mount Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378991}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|en|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|WD=Q386661|lt=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō, Tochigi}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin|en|Kōshin|WD=Q3082157|lt=Kōshin faith}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申待}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三尸|1=Three Corpses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9380693}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Shangdi|en|Shangdi|WD=Q2714875|lt=Tentei}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090037}}, {{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|en|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|WD=Q17004306|lt=Batō Kannon}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (申)}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three Wise Monkeys}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|1=itabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532699}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, dating to {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei (Japan)}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō|en|Shōhō|WD=Q1146192|lt=Shōhō (Japan)}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei era}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|1=Aihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042878}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|1=Takahashi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11671340}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|1=Shimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20044858}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[:en:Heavenly Emperor|Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[:en:Batō Kannon|Batō Kannon]], or [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three wise monkeys}} ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[:en:zodiac sign|zodiac sign]] of the monkey ({{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (zodiac)}}), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}} to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}}, while they are less common in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} within the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}, despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from {{ill|1471|en|1471|WD=Q6577|lt=1471}} in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. The earliest depiction of [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} stone from {{ill|1647|en|1647|WD=Q6935|lt=1647}}, while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (early {{ill|17th century|en|17th century|WD=Q7016|lt=17th century}}). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[:en:Amida Buddha|Amida Buddha]] and {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}, but [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[:en:Toyooka|Toyooka]]’s {{ill|Tanto|en|Tanto|WD=Q7683849|lt=Tanto}} district. Most date from the {{ill|18th century|en|18th century|WD=Q7015|lt=18th century}} to the early {{ill|20th century|en|20th century|WD=Q6927|lt=20th century}}<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[:en:postwar economic boom|postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|1=Mount Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378991}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|en|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|WD=Q386661|lt=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō, Tochigi}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin|en|Kōshin|WD=Q3082157|lt=Kōshin faith}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申待}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三尸|1=Three Corpses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9380693}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Shangdi|en|Shangdi|WD=Q2714875|lt=Tentei}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090037}}, {{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|en|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|WD=Q17004306|lt=Batō Kannon}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (申)}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three Wise Monkeys}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|1=itabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532699}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, dating to {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei (Japan)}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō|en|Shōhō|WD=Q1146192|lt=Shōhō (Japan)}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei era}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|1=Aihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042878}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|1=Takahashi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11671340}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|1=Shimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20044858}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]]
{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[:en:Heavenly Emperor|Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[:en:Batō Kannon|Batō Kannon]], or [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three wise monkeys}} ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[:en:zodiac sign|zodiac sign]] of the monkey ({{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (zodiac)}}), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}} to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}}, while they are less common in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} within the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}, despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from {{ill|1471|en|1471|WD=Q6577|lt=1471}} in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. The earliest depiction of [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} stone from {{ill|1647|en|1647|WD=Q6935|lt=1647}}, while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (early {{ill|17th century|en|17th century|WD=Q7016|lt=17th century}}). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[:en:Amida Buddha|Amida Buddha]] and {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}, but [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[:en:Toyooka|Toyooka]]’s {{ill|Tanto|en|Tanto|WD=Q7683849|lt=Tanto}} district. Most date from the {{ill|18th century|en|18th century|WD=Q7015|lt=18th century}} to the early {{ill|20th century|en|20th century|WD=Q6927|lt=20th century}}<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[:en:postwar economic boom|postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
== Gallery == <gallery> Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Umi, Fukuoka|en|Umi, Fukuoka|WD=Q945518|lt=Umi, Fukuoka}}. Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima, Fukuoka|WD=Q1011145|lt=Itoshima, Fukuoka}}. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Izushi, Hyōgo|en|Izushi, Hyōgo|WD=Q6102453|lt=Izushi, Hyōgo}}. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[:en:Narashino, Chiba|Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ({{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|en|Fukaya, Saitama|WD=Q734532|lt=Fukaya, Saitama}}). </gallery>

== Kōshin-tō == [[File:Kōshinkuyōtō, Iseyama, Fujisawa, Kanagawa.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed at {{ill|Iseyama Park|ja|伊勢山 (藤沢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Park|1=Mount Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378991}} in {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|en|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|WD=Q386661|lt=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, on August 18, 2017]] [[File:Kosinto0091.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Photographed in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō, Tochigi}}]]
'''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔) or '''Kōshin-zuka''' (庚申塚) is a stone monument erected as part of {{ill|Kōshin|en|Kōshin|WD=Q3082157|lt=Kōshin faith}}, which originated in {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} and was transmitted from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} to Japan. These towers were often built as commemorations for completing 18 rounds of {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申待}} over three years. Because they were placed atop mounds, they were also called '''Kōshin-zuka''', and due to the accompanying rituals, they were sometimes referred to as '''Kōshin Kuyō-tō''' (庚申供養塔).
{{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}} (or Kōshin-kō) was a belief that, on the night of a {{ill|Kōshin day|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin day|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, the {{ill|Sanshi|ja|三尸虫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三尸|1=Three Corpses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9380693}} (Three Corpses) within a person’s body would ascend to {{ill|Shangdi|en|Shangdi|WD=Q2714875|lt=Tentei}} while they slept and report their misdeeds. To prevent this, believers would remain awake throughout the night, performing rituals for Tentei, {{ill|Saratobi|ja|猿田彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saratobi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090037}}, {{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|en|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|WD=Q17004306|lt=Batō Kannon}}, or {{ill|Shōmen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōmen Kongō|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}}, engaging in prayers or festivities.
The appearance of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Since the Chinese zodiac sign {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (申)}} is associated with monkeys, many feature carvings of the {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three Wise Monkeys}}, along with inscriptions of village names and Kōshin-kō participants. Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|1=itabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532699}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, dating to {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei (Japan)}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō|en|Shōhō|WD=Q1146192|lt=Shōhō (Japan)}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei era}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|1=Aihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042878}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|1=Takahashi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11671340}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|1=Shimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20044858}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kōshin-tō]][[ja:庚申塔]]
{{Citation needed|date=January 2025}} '''Kōshin-tō''' (庚申塔), also called '''Kōshinzuka''' (庚申塚), are stone towers or mounds originating from the [[Taoism|Daoist]]-inspired [[Kōshin|Kōshin faith]] (庚申信仰) brought to Japan from {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. These towers are often erected to commemorate the Completion tier 2]] (庚申待, Kōshin-machi) held 18 times over three years. Because they were built atop mounds, they are also referred to as '''Kōshinzuka''', and due to the associated rituals, they are also called '''Kōshin Kuyōtō''' (庚申供養塔).
The [[Kōshin vigil]] involves staying awake through the night of [[Kōshin]] days to prevent the [[Sanshi]] (三尸虫), parasitic spirits believed to inhabit the human body, from reporting one’s misdeeds to the [[:en:Heavenly Emperor|Heavenly Emperor]]. To avert this, people worship deities such as [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[:en:Batō Kannon|Batō Kannon]], or [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and perform devotional or celebratory activities.
The design of Kōshin-tō varies widely. Common motifs include {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three wise monkeys}} ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil"), symbols associated with the [[:en:zodiac sign|zodiac sign]] of the monkey ({{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|WD=Q740674|lt=Monkey (zodiac)}}), or inscriptions of participant names from the vigil. [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] images, regarded as the patron deity of Kōshin in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}, or depictions of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]] in [[Shinto]] contexts, are frequently featured. Some Kōshin-tō along roads also serve as waymarkers or are erected as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}} to protect villages, often placed at boundaries or intersections.
Kōshin-tō can be found throughout Japan, except {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, with significant regional variations in numbers. For example, many exist in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}}, while they are less common in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} within the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}}, despite these areas’ centrality in Japan’s religious history. The oldest surviving Kōshin-tō is a [[Kōshin banpi]] (庚申板碑) from {{ill|1471|en|1471|WD=Q6577|lt=1471}} in {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}. The earliest depiction of [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] is found on a {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} stone from {{ill|1647|en|1647|WD=Q6935|lt=1647}}, while a wooden statue from [[Nara]]’s [[Tōdai-ji]] dates back to the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == The widespread construction of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (early {{ill|17th century|en|17th century|WD=Q7016|lt=17th century}}). Early towers featured diverse deities such as [[:en:Amida Buddha|Amida Buddha]] and {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}, but [[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] later became the dominant figure. In the mid to late Edo period, inscriptions with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became increasingly common.
In {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō have been documented in [[:en:Toyooka|Toyooka]]’s {{ill|Tanto|en|Tanto|WD=Q7683849|lt=Tanto}} district. Most date from the {{ill|18th century|en|18th century|WD=Q7015|lt=18th century}} to the early {{ill|20th century|en|20th century|WD=Q6927|lt=20th century}}<ref>『但東の庚申塔』但東町教育委員会, 1999, pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the government criticized Kōshin beliefs as superstition, leading to the removal of many towers, especially along roads. Later, urban development during Japan’s [[:en:postwar economic boom|postwar economic boom]] further reduced their numbers. Remaining Kōshin-tō are often found in temple grounds or private lands, where they were relocated for preservation.
== Gallery == <gallery> Image:庚申塔 UP200607171855.JPG|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Umi, Fukuoka|en|Umi, Fukuoka|WD=Q945518|lt=Umi, Fukuoka}}. Image:庚申塔 UP200607171910.JPG|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima, Fukuoka|WD=Q1011145|lt=Itoshima, Fukuoka}}. Image:出石町奥山の庚申塔と地蔵.jpg|Kōshin-tō in {{ill|Izushi, Hyōgo|en|Izushi, Hyōgo|WD=Q6102453|lt=Izushi, Hyōgo}}. Image:Koshin pillar.jpg|[[:en:Shōmen Kongō|Shōmen Kongō]] and three wise monkeys ([[:en:Narashino, Chiba|Narashino, Chiba]]). Image:Hyaku Kōshin.JPG|Cluster of Kōshin-tō ({{ill|Fukaya, Saitama|en|Fukaya, Saitama|WD=Q734532|lt=Fukaya, Saitama}}). </gallery>
== 3D Models == <gallery> File:庚申塔（八雲神社）.stl|Kōshin-tō from [[Yagumo Shrine]] (1770, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}). </gallery>

Due to Buddhist influence, many feature carvings of Shōmen Kongō, while in Shinto contexts, they depict Saratobi. Some Kōshin-tō served as road markers, while others functioned as {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, marking village boundaries.
Kōshin-tō are found across Japan except for {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, though their distribution varies. The Kanto region has an especially high concentration, whereas fewer are found in {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, despite major Kōshin faith centers in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}. The oldest known Kōshin-tō is a {{ill|Banbi|ja|板碑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banbi|1=itabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11532699}} from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, dating to {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei (Japan)}} 3 (1471). Early forms included banbi and {{ill|Sekidō|ja|石幢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekidō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, while the oldest known Shōmen Kongō carving, from {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, dates to {{ill|Shōhō|en|Shōhō|WD=Q1146192|lt=Shōhō (Japan)}} 4 (1647). Additionally, {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}} in Nara owns a wooden Shōmen Kongō statue from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
== History == Widespread erection of Kōshin-tō began in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, particularly after the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei era}}. Throughout the early modern period, numerous Kōshin-tō were constructed. Initially, various deities such as Amida and {{ill|Jizō|ja|地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}} appeared alongside the Three Monkeys and Shōmen Kongō, but Shōmen Kongō gradually became the dominant figure. By the mid-to-late Edo period, simpler stone tablets inscribed with just "Kōshin-tō" or "Kōshin" became more common.
In {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県豊岡市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyooka, Hyōgo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 77 stone Kōshin-tō were documented (35 in former {{ill|Aibashi Village|ja|合橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aibashi Village|1=Aihashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042878}}, 21 in {{ill|Takahashi Village|ja|高橋村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Village|1=Takahashi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11671340}}, 21 in {{ill|Shibomo Village|ja|資母村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibomo Village|1=Shimo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20044858}}), most built between the 18th and early 20th centuries<ref>但東町教育委員会『但東の庚申塔』、1999th year 、pp.6-7</ref>.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, the government deemed Kōshin faith superstitious and removed many Kōshin-tō along roadsides. Further urbanization and road expansion during Japan’s post-war economic boom resulted in the destruction or relocation of most remaining towers.
Today, most surviving Kōshin-tō have been relocated to temple precincts or private lands, while some, located in rural crossroads and village entrances, remain intact.
